body.stem: What is the normal adult resting heart rate range?
body.explanation: Normal adult resting heart rate is 60–100 bpm. Below 60 = bradycardia; above 100 = tachycardia. Athletes may have rates 40–60 bpm as a normal variant.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.stem: What blood pressure reading is classified as normal per the 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines?
body.explanation: Normal BP is < 120/80 mmHg. Elevated is 120–129 systolic (normal diastolic). Stage 1 HTN: 130–139/80–89. Stage 2 HTN: ≥ 140/90.

sourceCitation: 2017 ACC/AHA High Blood Pressure Guidelines; Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.stem: What is the normal adult respiratory rate range?
body.explanation: Normal adult RR is 12–20 breaths/min. < 12 = bradypnea; > 20 = tachypnea. Assess rate, depth, rhythm, and effort. Count for a full 60 seconds for accuracy.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.stem: What is the normal oral body temperature range for an adult?
body.explanation: Normal oral temperature is approximately 97.8–99.1°F (36.5–37.3°C). Values vary by time of day, age, and route (rectal > oral > axillary by ~0.5–1°F each). Fever is generally ≥ 100.4°F (38.0°C).

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.stem: What is the normal oxygen saturation (SpO2) range for a healthy adult?
body.explanation: Normal SpO2 is ≥ 95% in healthy adults at sea level. SpO2 88–92% may be acceptable in chronic COPD patients (hypoxic drive). SpO2 < 90% indicates hypoxemia requiring intervention.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.prompt: Describe the Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) for pain assessment. What scores require nursing intervention?
rubric: 0–10 scale where 0 = no pain, 10 = worst imaginable pain
0 = no pain; 1–3 = mild; 4–6 = moderate; 7–10 = severe
Moderate to severe pain (≥ 4) warrants intervention per most protocols
body.modelAnswer: The NRS is a 0–10 scale: 0 = no pain, 10 = worst imaginable pain. Mild pain is 1–3, moderate is 4–6, and severe is 7–10. A score of 4 or above typically warrants pharmacological or non-pharmacological intervention based on patient goals and clinical context.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: PERRLA is a documentation acronym for pupil assessment. It stands for: Pupils {{blank}}, {{blank}}, {{blank}} to {{blank}}, and {{blank}}.
body.hint: Used in neurological assessments — abnormal findings may indicate increased ICP or cranial nerve dysfunction

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.prompt: Describe the primary survey (ABCDE approach) used in rapid patient assessment.
rubric: A — Airway: is it patent? Any obstruction?
B — Breathing: rate, depth, effort, breath sounds
C — Circulation: pulse, BP, skin color/temperature/moisture, bleeding
D — Disability: level of consciousness (AVPU or GCS), pupils
E — Exposure/Environment: remove clothing to assess, prevent hypothermia
body.modelAnswer: The ABCDE primary survey: A-Airway (patent?), B-Breathing (rate, quality, effort), C-Circulation (pulse, BP, perfusion), D-Disability (neurological status, pupils), E-Exposure (full body assessment while maintaining warmth). Life-threatening problems at each step are addressed before proceeding.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.front: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a drop in systolic BP of ≥ {{blank}} mmHg or diastolic BP of ≥ {{blank}} mmHg within 3 minutes of standing, often accompanied by dizziness.
body.hint: Also called postural hypotension — a fall risk! Assess after lying → sitting → standing transitions.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.stem: What is the normal serum sodium (Na+) range?
body.explanation: Normal serum sodium is 135–145 mEq/L. Hyponatremia < 135, hypernatremia > 145. Sodium is the primary extracellular cation and the major determinant of plasma osmolality.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the normal serum potassium (K+) range?
body.explanation: Normal serum K+ is 3.5–5.0 mEq/L. Hypokalemia < 3.5 (risk of dysrhythmias, muscle weakness); hyperkalemia > 5.0 (risk of fatal dysrhythmias). Notify provider if K+ < 3.5 or > 5.5 mEq/L.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the normal fasting blood glucose range?
body.explanation: Normal fasting glucose is 70–100 mg/dL. Prediabetes: 100–125 mg/dL. Diabetes: ≥ 126 mg/dL on two occasions. Hypoglycemia: < 70 mg/dL (symptomatic < 54 mg/dL).

sourceCitation: ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes 2024; Saunders NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the general normal range for serum creatinine in adults?
body.explanation: Normal creatinine is approximately 0.6–1.2 mg/dL; values vary by sex, age, and muscle mass. Women and older adults typically have lower values. Elevated creatinine indicates reduced renal filtration. NOTE: ranges vary by laboratory — verify against your institution's reference range.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the general normal range for blood urea nitrogen (BUN)?
body.explanation: Normal BUN is approximately 7–25 mg/dL. Elevated BUN (>25) may indicate renal dysfunction, dehydration, high-protein diet, or GI bleeding. BUN:creatinine ratio > 20:1 suggests prerenal cause. NOTE: ranges vary by lab.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the normal white blood cell (WBC) count range for adults?
body.explanation: Normal adult WBC is 4,500–11,000 cells/mcL (some sources: 4,000–10,000). Leukocytosis (> 11,000) suggests infection/inflammation; leukopenia (< 4,500) indicates immune suppression risk.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the normal hemoglobin range for adult females?
body.explanation: Normal hemoglobin for adult females is 12.0–16.0 g/dL; for adult males it is 13.5–17.5 g/dL. Values below the lower limit indicate anemia. NOTE: ranges vary by lab and altitude.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: What is the normal platelet count range?
body.explanation: Normal platelets: 150,000–400,000/mcL. Thrombocytopenia < 150,000 (bleeding risk; < 50,000 significant; < 20,000 spontaneous bleeding). Thrombocytosis > 450,000 (clot risk). HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia) causes a drop ≥ 50% from baseline.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.front: Crackles (rales) are discontinuous, {{blank}} lung sounds produced by fluid in the alveoli or small airways, associated with conditions such as {{blank}} and pulmonary edema.
body.hint: Fine crackles: soft, high-pitched (end-inspiratory). Coarse crackles: louder, lower-pitched (throughout inspiration). Not cleared by coughing.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Wheezes are continuous, {{blank}}-pitched musical sounds caused by narrowed airways. They are associated with {{blank}} and asthma.
body.hint: Wheezes occur during expiration (or both phases in severe bronchospasm). Inspiratory wheezes are called stridor when in upper airway.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Rhonchi are continuous, {{blank}}-pitched sounds caused by secretions in the larger airways. Unlike crackles, they often {{blank}} with coughing.
body.hint: Rhonchi = snoring-like quality. If they clear with coughing, secretion management is the priority.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.prompt: Describe how to assess bowel sounds. What is normal? What findings require immediate notification?
rubric: Auscultate all four quadrants for at least 1–2 minutes each before documenting absent bowel sounds
Normal: gurgling sounds 5–30 per minute (or 5–35/min by some sources)
Hypoactive: 1–4/min (post-surgery, ileus, narcotics)
Hyperactive: > 30/min (diarrhea, early obstruction, GI bleeding)
Absent: no sounds after 5 minutes of listening per quadrant — report to provider (possible ileus or obstruction)
body.modelAnswer: Auscultate all four abdominal quadrants before percussion or palpation. Normal bowel sounds occur 5–30 times per minute as high-pitched gurgles. Listen for 5 minutes before calling sounds absent. Absent or hypoactive sounds suggest ileus; hyperactive ('rushing') sounds may indicate early obstruction or diarrhea — both require provider notification.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Normal capillary refill time in adults is {{blank}} seconds. A prolonged refill time suggests {{blank}} perfusion.
body.hint: Press nail bed until blanched, release, and count seconds until color returns. Temperature and age affect reliability.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: To assess for jugular venous distension (JVD), position the patient at a {{blank}}° angle. JVD is visible above the clavicle when it exceeds {{blank}} cm above the sternal angle.
body.hint: JVD is a sign of elevated central venous pressure — seen in right-sided heart failure and cardiac tamponade

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.prompt: List the three components of the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and the total score range.
rubric: Eye opening: scored 1–4 (1=none, 2=to pain, 3=to voice, 4=spontaneous)
Verbal response: scored 1–5 (1=none, 2=sounds, 3=words, 4=confused, 5=oriented)
Motor response: scored 1–6 (1=none, 2=extension, 3=abnormal flexion, 4=withdrawal, 5=localizes, 6=obeys)
Total range: 3–15; ≤ 8 = severe brain injury, intubation considered
body.modelAnswer: GCS has three components: Eye opening (1–4), Verbal response (1–5), and Motor response (1–6). Total score ranges from 3 (deep coma/death) to 15 (fully alert). A score ≤ 8 indicates severe impairment requiring airway protection.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.prompt: Describe the 0–4 pulse quality grading scale.
rubric: 0 = absent (no pulse)
1+ = weak/thready (barely palpable)
2+ = normal (expected)
3+ = full/bounding (increased)
4+ = bounding (forceful; may indicate high cardiac output)
body.modelAnswer: Pulses are graded 0–4+: 0=absent, 1+=weak/thready, 2+=normal, 3+=full/bounding, 4+=excessively bounding. Document the scale used at your institution. A 1+ or absent pulse requires immediate provider notification.

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry
